title: Project Overview
text: This website is meant to aid collaboration between multiple agencies and field technicians for the purposes of data collection and data interpretation. This website will coordinate surveys and highlight progress made on a weekly to bi-weekly update schedule.

title: Point Counts
text:
Avian communities are often sampled with point count surveys, and for good reason. Point counts in this study will provide information on the grater cavity-nesting bird community which is important for understanding competition and community assemblages as they relate to environmental variables. Below are some preliminary results on each of the four sites with simple models using the spAbundance package in R. These models vary detection probability by species only and provide estimates on species abundance with associated confidence intervals.











This plot (above) shows the abundance estimates for cavity nesting species at each study site (separated by vertical dashed lines). This data represents only March and April 2025 since that’s what we have entered into excel thus far. Species estimates are shown with their corresponding 95% credible intervals.

title: Project Overview
text: This website is meant to aid collaboration between multiple agencies and field technicians for the purposes of data collection and data interpretation. This website will coordinate surveys and highlight progress made on a weekly to bi-weekly update schedule.

title: Project Overview
text:
Project Description
There are seven year-round resident woodpeckers in the southeastern United States. Like most woodpeckers, these species exhibit similar forms and exist in similar environments. Under competative exclusion, it is unlikely that these similar species should be able to coexist unless they utilize separate parts of the environment. Most previous research has focused on this ‘niche partitioning’ of their nesting habitat but little work has been done on how they partition foraging habitat.
This work focuses on the changes that occur in woodpecker communities after prescribed fire among the seven coocurring species in southeastern pine forests.



all eight woodpeckers (yellow-bellied sapsuckers migrate and are absent during breeding season)


This website was updated:
May 31, 2025

title: Habitat Assessment
text:
Habitat variables were obtained to explain the patterns in foraging behavior of each species as an alternative hypothesis. Habitat metrics also provide context for the study and makes it more translatable to other systems.
We collected information on dead trees (snags), burn history, and habitat structure.





Four example plots, one from each study site, displaying stratified points of canopy cover (circles) and snag locations (triangles). Snags are colored by decay class where increasing number represents more decayed trees.





Terrestrial laser scanning


To date, we have obtained  635  scans across all plots.


Terrestrial laser scanning (similar to LiDAR) is capable of deriving 200+ metrics from the environment in under two minutes by shooting lasers out in many directions and retrieving the resulting distance to conceptualize the structure of the environment. To date, we have obtained 635 scans across all plots. Belo are the results from those scans.




Table: Habitat variables for each site.

|site_abb | mean_tree_density|   BA_mean| max_canopy_height| pine_percent|geometry                       |
|:--------|-----------------:|---------:|-----------------:|------------:|:------------------------------|

|GREN     |         0.0301542| 12.112676|          26.14298|     86.18024|MULTIPOINT ((782429 3418483... |
|LIVI     |         0.0113447|  4.982159|          15.33614|     78.38713|MULTIPOINT ((816095.1 33936... |
|TTRS     |         0.0137810|  5.965745|          18.01770|     85.96535|MULTIPOINT ((763534.5 33953... |




Snag data









There’s a lot of life after death when thinking about dead trees. In the early stages of death, dead trees may become hot-spots of food for woodpeckers as beetle larvae eat at the more susceptible wood. But after a few months, this resource decreases as beetles mature and consume most of the nutritious parts of the tree. Then, over many years the tree decomposes and become a nesting resource for cavity-nesting birds and other taxons.
Here, we describe the quality and quantity of these snag resources. There are many ways to quantify a dead tree including decay class, diameter at breast height (DBH), amount of dead wood, amount of bark, and species. This diversity of snag characteristics within a plot my lead to more diversity within the plot. Thus, I will reduce this dimensionality in the data using multivariate statistics. This will result in a one or two metrics that can be used for further analysis.
We have sampled most of all plots for the presence of dead trees. On average, there are 23.25 snags per plot (range: 0 - 54) for a total of 744 snags recorded across all plots.









We also want to know something about the diversity of these snags that are in each plot. Are all snags created equal? As mentioned before, probably not. We derive a diversity metric from all the snag’s qualities within a plot. How big is it, how decayed (old) is it, is it a pine or hardwood, and use these to get a Gower distance for the plots.





Snag diversity among plots given the number of unique cavity-nesting birds seen at a plot (richness, A) and the proportion of shared species between plot (0 = all species are shared, 1 = no species in common (AKA species turnover, B).




It appears that the diversity of snags does not correlate strongly to the number of species (Fig 3A) nor to the species shared between plots (Fig 3B). This makes some sense if particular snags are more useful as opposed to others. Imagine a tree that is not far along in its decay next to another snag that has decayed for much longer. The firsttree is likely thicker, with soft rotten wood (good cavity substrate) while the well-decayed snag is likely short, contains no rotten wood and only a dense heartwood. I expect that the diversity of snags doesn’t reflect the number or variety of species at a plot, because only a particular kind of snag matters. The snags that make for good nesting substrates.

title: Woodpecker Observations
text:
August coming soon…
Now let’s see how this looks in multidimensional space. We use a principle coordinate analysis since we are dealing with categorical variables such as the percentage of time gleaning from live secondary branches compared to hawking. We are also interested in time since fire, but for now, we will plot by month.





Principle coordinate analysis (PCoA) on the foraging observations by species and month.
